quote: No. I I I wasn't interested in that. So I already had the the the the the discount, and then that's something that's trying to find out how much was it and everything.
stewart_read: John's opening reframe is the most important moment in the call. He's not a motivated solar buyer — he's a passive incentive-browser. The schema marks interest_in_solar as 'THE EMOTIONAL ANCHOR' and specifies that 'depth matters more than the answer' and 'top setters spend 60+ seconds here.' Joel spends under 20 seconds, mirrors the low-investment framing, and moves on. The strategic read — that Joel was deliberately accepting the low frame to build trust before re-engaging — is not supported; there is no later re-engagement attempt. This is a reactive miss on the call's highest-leverage verify item.
alternative_hypothesis: Joel accepted the low-investment framing strategically to reduce resistance early, planning to re-anchor motivation once rapport was established.
coaching_implication: Show Joel the schema's depth-grading note on interest_in_solar. The question 'What had you interested in solar?' got a one-sentence answer and a box-check. The manager should ask Joel: what would you have done differently if you knew this was the emotional anchor? A suggested probe: 'So when you were looking at the incentives — was it more about the monthly savings, or was it the bill itself that was bothering you?' That question opens the bill-pain door without arguing with John's framing.

quote: Dang. Okay. Yeah. Those are up there for sure.
stewart_read: John has just disclosed a ~$300/month Dominion bill. This is the single strongest emotional anchor in the call. The schema's capture pattern is 'ask + acknowledge + flip' — Joel executes 'ask + acknowledge + move on.' The flip would have been: 'So you're paying Dominion $300 a month right now — that's the expense solar replaces, not adds to.' Instead, Joel says 'dang' and pivots to prior designs. The bill pain is real; the leverage is abandoned.
coaching_implication: This is the floor's universal coaching gap (schema notes zero flip instances across the corpus). Use this moment to introduce the objection_inversion mechanic. The manager doesn't need to teach a script — just ask Joel: 'After John told you his bill, what did you say next, and what were you trying to accomplish?' Then show him the flip: the bill is the expense solar replaces. The number John gave is the argument for solar, not a data point to file away.

stewart_read: Joel asks 'Is there anyone else on the title of the home with you at all?' and John's answer — 'It's been a while' — is ambiguous. It could mean he's been the sole owner for a while, or it could mean he hasn't thought about it in a while, or it could signal a life change (divorce, spousal loss). Joel responds with 'Okay. Sounds good.' and moves on without clarifying. The schema marks title/co-owner confirmation as required and critical for spouse coordination at button-up. This is an unresolved qualification item that could surface as a deal-killer at the closer stage.
alternative_hypothesis: Joel read 'it's been a while' as confirmation of sole ownership and moved on deliberately to avoid an awkward follow-up.
coaching_implication: The manager should flag this as an unresolved title question in the handoff brief. For coaching: Joel needs a simple clarifying follow-up when an answer is ambiguous — 'So it's just you on the title?' takes three seconds and closes the gap. The schema is explicit: if a spouse or co-owner is on the title, they must be on the appointment.

quote: I about six, eight months ago.
stewart_read: John discloses he looked into solar six to eight months ago. This is the highest-diagnostic moment in the call: something stopped him from moving forward. Joel's response is a generic equipment-improvement comment ('the equipment has gotten a lot better') rather than the obvious diagnostic question: 'What stopped you from moving forward at that point?' The answer to that question would have surfaced the real barrier — price, spouse, credit, skepticism — and given Joel something concrete to address or route to the closer.
alternative_hypothesis: Joel used the equipment comment as a soft curiosity-creation move, planning to diagnose the barrier later in the call.
coaching_implication: This is a standalone coaching moment. Ask Joel: 'When John told you he'd looked into it six months ago, what did you want to know next?' The answer is almost always 'what stopped him.' The manager should drill the diagnostic question: 'What stopped you from moving forward at that point?' — it's one sentence and it changes the entire second half of the call.

quote: Through the mail. Sometimes sometimes I don't get done at all.
stewart_read: John discloses two things simultaneously: he gets paper bills, and he sometimes doesn't receive them at all. The second disclosure is a direct signal that the paper-bill path is unreliable. The schema's alternative_paths section addresses exactly this scenario — when the standard bill-collection path is blocked, the rep should pivot to verbal read-back or defer to the specialist site visit. Joel ignores the 'sometimes I don't get them at all' signal entirely and doubles down on the paper-bill path ('if I could have you grab just one of those recent paper bills'). The result: John says he's not home, the call stalls, and the bill is never collected.
coaching_implication: Show Joel the schema's alternative_paths section. The 'sometimes I don't get them at all' line was the trigger to pivot — either to verbal read-back ('No problem — can you just tell me roughly how many kilowatt hours you used last month?') or to a Dominion online account lookup. Instead, Joel walked into the 'I'm not home' wall that was telegraphed two lines earlier. The manager should ask: 'What did John tell you about his bills before you asked him to grab one?'

quote: the equipment has gotten, you know, a lot better. So, yeah, I think it's gonna be awesome for you to just kinda see what the options look like nowadays.
stewart_read: Joel's response to the prior-inquiry disclosure is a vague equipment-improvement claim. This is not scope creep at the level the schema defines (explaining specs, debating operations), but it is a curiosity-creation failure: Joel is selling a generic 'things are better now' narrative rather than making John curious about his specific numbers. The schema's curiosity_over_features section is explicit — 'resist the urge to explain; focus on their bill.' The prior-inquiry moment called for a diagnostic question, not a feature-adjacent comment.
coaching_implication: This is a coaching moment about what curiosity-creation actually sounds like. The manager can contrast Joel's line with a suggested alternative: 'A lot has changed in six months — honestly, the numbers look different now. What was the main thing that made you hold off last time?' That question creates curiosity AND surfaces the barrier. Joel's version does neither.

quote: Yeah. I could give you a call kinda tomorrow in the morning if that works good for you, and we can just get the rest of the information we need.
stewart_read: The call ends with a soft callback — no locked time, no appointment structure, no button-up elements (no one-hour block, no computer/no-driving anchor, no 'is that fair' handshake, no text follow-up promise). The schema's button_up section marks all of these as required. 'Tomorrow in the morning' is not a locked time. The schema's analysis_directives.soft_outcome_distinction is explicit: 'callbacks WITH locked times vs we'll touch base — major delta on no-show rate.' This is firmly in the 'we'll touch base' category.
alternative_hypothesis: Joel deliberately kept the callback soft to avoid pressuring a low-engagement customer, planning to lock the time on the callback itself.
coaching_implication: The manager should walk Joel through the button_up checklist against this call — zero elements were completed. The coaching question is not 'why didn't you do the button-up' but 'what did you think you were booking?' If Joel thought this was a real appointment, he needs to see the no-show rate data on soft callbacks. If he knew it was soft, he needs to understand why locking a specific time ('I'll call you at 9 AM — does that work?') costs nothing and recovers significant no-show risk.

quote: I would say about 3¢ a month.
stewart_read: John's bill disclosure — clearly ~$300/month given the context and Joel's 'they kinda always been up there in 3 hundreds' follow-up — is a real pain signal. A $300/month Dominion bill is meaningful leverage. John also confirms this has been a recurring pattern ('Not always, but it didn't let me have been'). This is not a casual aside; it's the strongest qualification signal in the call. Joel captures it and drops it. The enthusiasm signal here is that John's bill pain is real and recent — this customer has a financial reason to move, even if his stated motivation was passive curiosity.
coaching_implication: The manager should use this moment to show Joel what an anchored bill looks like versus a filed bill. The $300 figure is the argument for solar — it's not data to collect and move past. The flip: 'So right now you're paying Dominion $300 a month — that's the expense we're looking to replace, not add to. That's what the design is built around.' Joel had the number; he just didn't use it.
